question: What is Sentinel and how does it work?
answer: Sentinel monitors notifications from any app on your phone. When a notification matches your configured keywords, it triggers a real-world alarm — even if your phone is on silent or the screen is off.

question: Does it work with the phone on silent?
answer: Yes. Sentinel can bypass silent and do-not-disturb modes when the alarm is triggered. You configure this per card, so you stay in full control.

question: What is a monitoring card?
answer: A card is a rule you create: choose an app, set keywords to match in the notification title and/or body, and pick what alarm to trigger. You can have up to 3 cards for free.

question: What smart devices does it support?
answer: Any device that accepts a simple HTTP GET request — Shelly relays, Tapo plugs, Home Assistant, or any custom endpoint. You provide the on/off URLs and Sentinel calls them when the alarm triggers.

question: What's included in Premium?
answer: Premium is a one-time purchase that unlocks unlimited monitoring cards, the smart device alarm type, and the ability to import cards shared by other users.

question: Does Sentinel collect or share my data?
answer: No. Sentinel processes everything locally on your device. No notification content, keywords, or personal data is ever sent to external servers.

question: The ringtone picker isn't showing my ringtones — what's wrong?
answer: This can happen if you've never set a ringtone on your device before. When Sentinel opens the ringtone picker, Android needs a default app to browse your system ringtones. If no app has been assigned yet, Android may show a chooser dialog asking you to pick one — and selecting the wrong app means the ringtone list won't appear correctly. The quickest fix is to set a ringtone once from your phone's Clock or Alarm app — Android will automatically associate the right app, and Sentinel's picker will work correctly from that point on. If you already selected the wrong app by mistake and tapped Always, you'll need to reset it manually under Settings → Apps → Default apps.

question: Why doesn't Sentinel see the body text of some notifications?
answer:
Some apps (especially smart-home and IoT apps like Blink) draw the notification body with a custom graphic layout instead of using Android's standard text field. What you see in the notification shade is an image rendered by the app — not a data field Sentinel can read through Android's public API. When this happens, Sentinel shows a small 'unusual format' hint in the Wizard Step 1 or when changing the app in Advanced mode.

What to do: configure the card starting from a notification the app has already delivered (Wizard → Step 1), don't fill the fields from scratch. That way you see exactly the text Sentinel can match on, not the richer preview Android shows you.

Rule of thumb for these apps: prefer keywords in the title, not the body. Blink, for example, sends a summary notification with the full text in the title for every motion event — a card with a keyword like 'motion' in the title will trigger reliably, while matching the body won't work because the body is only drawn graphically.

Known apps with this pattern so far: Blink Home Monitor (com.immediasemi.android.blink). Found another one? Report it via 'Report a bug' in Settings.
